summary:
The Remedello culture was a Chalcolithic (Copper Age) archaeological culture of northern Italy, centered on the Po Valley and named after a cemetery discovered at Remedello di Sopra in Brescia province. Flourishing approximately 3400–2400 BCE, the culture is characterized by flat inhumation cemeteries in which the dead were buried with distinctive grave goods including tanged copper daggers, flint arrowheads, stone battle-axes, and bead ornaments.

The Remedello people practiced mixed farming and animal husbandry, and their copper metalworking represents an important early chapter in Italian prehistory. The famous Alpine mummy known as [[event:1672|Ötzi the Iceman]], discovered in 1991, lived during the period of the Remedello culture and carried a copper axe of similar type to those found in Remedello graves. This connection has made Remedello an important reference point for understanding social complexity, violence, and long-distance exchange in Chalcolithic Europe.
